name: Foundations
description: Build the professional foundation you need to succeed in any field — from identity and technology to self-management and social impact.

units.name: Foundations 0: Basic WordPress
units.description: WordPress powers nearly half the web - understanding it opens the door to building almost anything online.
units.exemplar: A published WordPress post on the 11:11 Philosopher's Group site (philosophers.group). The page has a simple, clean theme with the site name as a linked header at the top. The post title is "My WordPress Primer" in large heading text, followed by four sections separated by horizontal rules: "I Tried WordPress Playground and Here's What I Learned," "What the Four Freedoms Actually Mean," "How WordPress Got Here," and "Why Playground Is a Good Way to Learn." The Four Freedoms section uses a bulleted list with bold lead-ins. The history section is also a bulleted list with bold years. The post ends with a "Questions?" section and a link to join a Slack workspace. The overall tone is first-person and conversational — it reads like a student reflecting on what they did and what surprised them.
learningObjectives: Can launch a WordPress Playground (https://playground.wordpress.net/) instance, navigate it, and publish a first post.
Can explain the four freedoms of WordPress and why they matter
Can describe key moments in the history of WordPress
Can explain how WordPress Playground supports learning, testing, and experimentation through direct exploration

units.name: Foundations 1: Identity
units.description: Knowing why you do what you do helps you make better decisions about where to go next.
units.exemplar: Before this module, I thought my professional purpose was just 'to be a software engineer.' Now, I know it's deeper. I identified my core interests (problem-solving, collaboration), values (transparency, community), and strengths (systems thinking, clear writing) and realized my true purpose is 'to build accessible software solutions that empower non-profits to manage their data transparently.' This reframed everything. I drafted a purpose statement for my LinkedIn bio—a formal, concise version—and a separate, more detailed version for a scholarship application. The revision process, focusing on tone and avoiding jargon, was key to ensuring both audiences understood not just what I do, but why I do it. This whole exercise gave me clarity on which job opportunities to pursue next.
learningObjectives: Can identify the interests, values, and strengths that shape your professional purpose
Can explain how audience and context influence the communication of professional purpose
Can draft a written statement of professional motivation and direction
Can revise a statement of purpose for clarity, tone, and accessibility

units.name: Foundations 2: Technology
units.description: Mapping the tools you know and the ones you need keeps your learning focused and intentional.
units.exemplar: My target role is 'AI Prompt Engineer' in the digital marketing space. I mapped out the essential tools: Known—Google Workspace, basic Python, and Canva. Needed to Learn—specific LLMs like GPT-4 and Claude 3, prompt chaining frameworks, and data visualization libraries like Matplotlib. I organized this into a personal technology stack, sectioned into 'Current Proficiency,' 'In-Progress Learning,' and 'Future Exploration.' I recognized that the most effective way for me to learn the LLM frameworks is through hands-on practice via self-contained projects, not just tutorials. My learning roadmap now prioritizes a two-week deep dive into a specific prompt chaining framework, which is the biggest gap between my current knowledge and the industry's expectations.
learningObjectives: Can identify the main tools, platforms, and systems relevant to your target role or field
Can distinguish between the technologies you already know and those you still need to learn
Can recognize effective ways of learning new technologies
Can organize relevant technologies into a personal technology stack and learning roadmap

units.name: Foundations 3: Information
units.description: Poor organization is the silent killer of good work — learning to manage information makes everything else easier.
units.exemplar: I always struggled with finding old project files, which I now realize is a common problem caused by weak organization, leading to duplicated effort and missed deadlines. For my target field, 'UX Research,' I learned that information is systematically organized by project phases (Discovery, Ideation, Testing) and user groups, often in shared Notion databases with standardized naming conventions ([PROJECT_ID]-[PHASE]-[ARTIFACT_TYPE]). My current habit was saving everything to a giant 'Research' folder with vague names. By evaluating this gap, I created a new system in my Drive that mirrors the field's structure. For example, my notes are now organized and tagged in a central repository, making retrieval immediate and efficient. This focus on organization strategies has already made my personal learning process smoother.
learningObjectives: Can explain why organization strategies matter in work and learning
Can recognize common problems caused by weak organization
Can identify how your target field organizes information, tasks, and resources
Can describe your current habits for organizing information, tasks, and resources
Can evaluate the gaps between your current habits and the organizational expectations of your field

units.name: Foundations 4: Self-Management
units.description: Understanding your own focus patterns is the first step to working consistently without burning out.
units.exemplar: I discovered my personal pattern is 'perfectionist paralysis'—I avoid starting big tasks until I have a perfect plan, which is a huge barrier to getting started. My common distraction is switching between email and my work document every 10 minutes. To address this, I produced a simple personal focus plan based on the Pomodoro Technique. For task initiation, I apply the '5-minute rule' (just work for five minutes). For sustained attention, I use a physical timer and block my email during 25-minute sprints. If interrupted, my recovery strategy is a quick 'mindful breath' and checking my plan before re-engaging. This plan has dramatically improved my follow-through on complex assignments.
learningObjectives: Can recognize personal patterns that affect focus and follow-through
Can identify common distractions and barriers to getting started
Can apply strategies that support task initiation and sustained attention
Can apply strategies to recover focus after interruption or distraction
Can produce a simple personal focus plan

units.name: Foundations 5: Social Impact
units.description: Connecting your work to something bigger than yourself gives it direction and staying power.
units.exemplar: I identified the community I want to support as local food banks in my city, and the challenge is their outdated inventory management, which leads to significant food waste. The positive change I want to contribute is building a simple, free, open-source inventory app to help them track donations efficiently. My personal value of 'sustainability' and my goal of becoming a software developer are directly connected to this desired impact. I articulated a social impact goal: 'By the end of the year, I will deploy a minimum viable inventory application that reduces food waste by 10% across three local food banks, directly applying my full-stack development skills to a community need.' This goal links the community's problem (waste) with my personal purpose (building tech solutions).
learningObjectives: Can identify a community, group, or issue you want your work to support
Can explain a problem or challenge affecting that community, group, or issue
Can describe the kind of positive change you want to contribute to
Can connect your desired impact to your personal values and goals
Can articulate a social impact goal that connects community need, desired change, and personal purpose

units.name: Foundations 6: Influence
units.description: Knowing who shapes your field helps you learn faster and find your place in the conversation.
units.exemplar: To understand the 'EdTech Product Management' field, I synthesized an influence map. Key organizations shaping standards include the ISTE and venture capital firms like GSV Ventures. The individuals whose ideas I follow are Sal Khan (for vision) and Shreyas Doshi (for practical PM strategy). The main channels where ideas are shaped and shared are private Slack communities for PMs and specific industry newsletters like 'The EdTech Review.' I evaluated their relevance: ISTE is essential for understanding policy, while Doshi is crucial for tactical skill development. By connecting these influencers to my professional foundation (my identity as a builder of accessible learning tools), I now know exactly where to focus my networking and learning efforts. The map serves as a living document to guide my professional reading.
learningObjectives: Can identify organizations that shape standards, opportunities, or direction in your target field
Can identify individuals whose ideas, work, or reputation influence that field
Can recognize the platforms, communities, or channels where ideas in the field are shaped and shared
Can evaluate the relevance of key people and organizations to your own goals and learning needs
Can synthesize key field influencers into a coherent influence map connected to your broader professional foundation
